coredata.dc:title: The effect and mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic fungicidal activity on nitrogen doped carbon dots-modified titanium dioxide

Gu, Lingwen
Liu, Ke
Lin, Jing
Wang, Qian
Yu, Bing

Fu, Xueyun
Li, Daohao
Zhao, Guiqiu
Li, Cui
coredata.dc:description: The photocatalytic antifungal strategy has attracted much attention in the field of fungicidal therapy due to its efficient therapeutic efficacy and avoidance of drug resistance. However, exploring safe and efficient photocatalysts remains an urgent challenge. In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) combined with titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanomaterials were synthesized by a hydrothermal method to exert a photocatalytic antifungal effect under sunlight irradiation. Compared with carbon dots/TiO2 without N-doped modification, employing N doping to fabricate N-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst could further markedly optimize the bandgap structure of TiO2, improve the absorption of the solar spectrum and accelerate the electron-hole pairs separation, thereby greatly enhancing the photocatalytic reaction and antifungal effect. Remarkably, in the photocatalytic antifungal reaction, N-CDs/TiO2 produces more oxygen radicals such as •OH and •O2 − to rapidly hinder the germination of Aspergillus fumigatus hyphae and the formation of fungal biofilm. Additionally, the incorporation of N-CDs and TiO2 possesses the great biocompatibility. Overall, this study proves that employing N-CDs/TiO2 nanomaterials can be a promising and efficient therapeutic method against fungal infections.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Titanium dioxide
Photocatalysis
Fungicide

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic Illustration of N-CDs/TiO2 synthesis. (b) HRTEM images of N-CDs and (c–e) N-CDs/TiO2. (f) EDS elemental mapping of N-CDs/TiO2 for C, N, O, and Ti.

(a) XRD patterns of TiO2, N-CDs/TiO2, CDs/TiO2, N-CDs, and CDs. (b) FT-IR spectra at a wavelength range of 500–4000 cm−1 of TiO2, N-CDs/TiO2, CDs/TiO2, N-CDs, and CDs. The high-resolution (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s, (e) O 1s, and (f) Ti 2p XPS spectra of N-CDs/TiO2.

(a) The mechanism of photocatalytic antifungal property in N-CDs/TiO2 against A. fumigatus. (b) The spread plate assays after CDs, N-CDs, TiO2, CDs/TiO2, and N-CDs/TiO2 treatments under dark and 5, 10, 15, 30 min of light irradiation. (c) CFW stained images of fungal hyphae after different treatments. (d) Images of fungal biofilms stained by crystal violet in the presence of different samples with light irradiation. (e) The calculated fungal mass after different treatments. (f) Quantification of biofilms after different treatments. (g) Quantification of living fungal colonies on the spread plate. (∗∗∗p < 0.001). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

EPR spectra of (a) DMPO-•OH and (b) DMPO-•O2 − adducts in N-CDs/TiO2, CDs/TiO2, and TiO2 under light irradiation. (c) The quantitative analysis and (d) optical images of photocatalytic antifungal property of N-CDs/TiO2 with the addition of different scavengers under dark and light irradiation.

(a) Photoluminescence emission spectra, (b) transient photocurrent response curves, (c) polarization curves, (d) the Mott-Schottky plots at different frequencies of 1000 Hz, 1500 Hz, and 2000 Hz, (e) the Tauc plots, and (f) the energy band structures of TiO2, CDs/TiO2, N-CDs/TiO2, CDs, and N-CDs under light irradiation.

CCK-8 assessments of (a) CDs, (b) N-CDs, (c) TiO2, (d) CDs/TiO2, and (e) N-CDs/TiO2 at different concentrations from 7.8 to 1000 μg/mL. (f) The Calcein-stained live cells and PI-stained dead cells images after CDs, N-CDs, TiO2, CDs/TiO2, and N-CDs/TiO2 treatments for 24 h. (g) Images of EdU staining (blue) in HCECs after different treatments. (∗∗p < 0.01; ∗∗∗p < 0.001). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The photocatalytic fungicidal mechanism of N-CDs/TiO2.

head.ce:title: The effect and mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic fungicidal activity on nitrogen doped carbon dots-modified titanium dioxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Ophthalmology, The Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University, No. 16 Jiangsu Road, Qingdao, Shandong Province, 266003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Ophthalmology, The Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University, No. 16 Jiangsu Road, Qingdao, Shandong Province, 266003, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, P. R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The photocatalytic antifungal strategy has attracted much attention in the field of fungicidal therapy due to its efficient therapeutic efficacy and avoidance of drug resistance. However, exploring safe and efficient photocatalysts remains an urgent challenge. In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) combined with titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanomaterials were synthesized by a hydrothermal method to exert a photocatalytic antifungal effect under sunlight irradiation. Compared with carbon dots/TiO2 without N-doped modification, employing N doping to fabricate N-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst could further markedly optimize the bandgap structure of TiO2, improve the absorption of the solar spectrum and accelerate the electron-hole pairs separation, thereby greatly enhancing the photocatalytic reaction and antifungal effect. Remarkably, in the photocatalytic antifungal reaction, N-CDs/TiO2 produces more oxygen radicals such as •OH and •O2 − to rapidly hinder the germination of Aspergillus fumigatus hyphae and the formation of fungal biofilm. Additionally, the incorporation of N-CDs and TiO2 possesses the great biocompatibility. Overall, this study proves that employing N-CDs/TiO2 nanomaterials can be a promising and efficient therapeutic method against fungal infections.

ce:section.ce:para: Fungal infections pose a significant global health challenge, particularly with a remarkable increase in the threat to immunocompromised populations [1]. Among the susceptible body parts, corneal infection caused by fungi is a highly damaging and prevalent ocular disease [2]. More than 60 % of fungal keratitis patients suffer from irreversible visual impairment and even blindness, which could lead to a significant loss of labor in society [3]. However, the efficacy of traditional antifungal drugs is restricted by the high toxicity and rapid development of drug resistance in treating fungal infectious diseases with high morbidity and poor prognosis [4,5]. Moreover, the high lethality of invasive fungal infections caused by Aspergillus and Candida further emphasizes the inadequacy of existing treatments [6]. Given the serious outcomes and inadequate management of fungal infections, discovering a novel and effective antifungal strategy with superior biocompatibility has become urgent.

Light-mediated antifungal strategy as an emerging therapy can destroy various fungi under light stimulation by generating multiple reactive oxygen radicals, which effectively reduces the uncontrolled use of irritating antifungal drugs and lowers the risk of fungal drug resistance [7,8]. Thus, photocatalytic reaction has been regarded as a highly efficient fungicidal method and has attracted much attention in the treatment of infectious diseases [9]. As one of the most widely distributed and non-polluting green energy sources, sunlight has been reported to be a great potential energy for application in the field of photocatalysis [10]. The preparation of photocatalysts that can better absorb sunlight for photocatalytic applications has attracted much attention. Recently, titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanoparticle is a widely used photocatalyst with high optical stability and oxidizability [11,12]. In a study of medical device coatings, TiO2-based photocatalytic nanoparticles could destroy mature biofilms to prevent implant-related infections [13]. However, due to its inherent wide bandgap and high electron-hole recombination, TiO2 only absorbs ultraviolet light, which accounts for less than 5 % of solar lights, leading to limited photocatalytic efficiency and antifungal effectiveness under sunlight [14,15]. Therefore, it is essential to explore a safe modified TiO2 technology with effective sunlight utilization and antifungal capacity.

Carbon dots are emerging carbon nanomaterials with diameters less than 10 nm. Due to the superior electron transport property, broad spectral absorption range, and easy surface modification, carbon dots have been widely studied in combining with various semiconductors as co-photocatalysts in the field of photocatalysis, bioimaging, and antimicrobial application [16,17]. Since the atomic orbitals of heteroatoms overlap with those of carbon atoms, the introduction of heteroatoms into the nanostructures of carbon dots can lead to significant changes in their electronic structures and nanostructures [18]. Thus, heteroatom doping can be considered as a simple and reliable method to improve the photocatalytic efficiency of carbon dots [19]. Previous studies confirmed that the doping of nitrogen (N) altered the electronic structure of carbon dots and produce additional active sites, which was contributed to more efficient electron-hole separation and reduces the recombination rate, thereby enhancing photocatalytic efficiency [7,20]. For instance, the study conducted by Chen et al. demonstrated that N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) combined with zinc oxide increased the photocatalytic degradation in water by introducing more active sites [21]. In addition, Ren et al. confirmed that N-doped carbon dots improved electron mobility and facilitated the carbon dioxide reduction reaction [22]. Therefore, carbon dots with N-doped, which has superior electron transition efficiency and great biocompatibility, can be considered as an optimal choice to modified TiO2 photocatalyst. Furthermore, whether the combination of carbon dots with N-doped and TiO2 can improve photocatalytic performance and antifungal effect has not been investigated yet.

Herein, the modification of TiO2 photocatalyst by N-CDs was developed to enhance photoreaction and exert photocatalytic antifungal property under sunlight against Aspergillus fumigatus (A. fumigatus). In this study, chitosan was used as the precursor to synthesize N-CDs and sodium alginate as the precursor to synthesize carbon dots without N-doped (CDs) by a hydrothermal method. And then, the prepared N-CDs and CDs were coupled with TiO2 nanoparticles to fabricate N-CDs/TiO2 and CDs/TiO2 photocatalytic materials. This research proves that the introduction of N doping into carbon dots can further optimize the bandgap structure of the photocatalytic system and facilitate faster electron transfer to the conduction band on the surface of TiO2, thus N-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst significantly enhanced the efficiency of photocatalytic reactions. Notably, in photocatalytic antifungal reactions, N-CDs/TiO2 is observed to efficiently limit the growth of A. fumigatus mycelia, destroy the fungal cell wall, and inhibit biofilms formation through generating higher levels of reactive oxygen species (ROSs). The coupling of N-CDs with TiO2 exhibits excellent biocompatibility. In summary, N-CDs/TiO2 can be considered as a promising innovative photocatalyst for treating fungal infections.

Carbon dots were synthesized by a hydrothermal method according to the previous research [23]. 1g chitosan (Macklin, Shanghai, China) was added into 25 mL distilled water and stirred with magnetic stirring. The chain structure of chitosan provided the carbon source for the formation of carbon dots, and its amino groups provided the N source. The mixture was placed into a Teflon autoclave at 200 °C for 5 h. After centrifuging at 10000 rpm for 15 min, the supernatant was filtered through an organic membrane (0.22 μm pore size) to remove unreacted substances and dialyzed for 24 h. The N-doped carbon dots were obtained by freeze-drying. The carbon dots without N-doped were synthesized using sodium alginate (Aladdin, Shanghai, China) instead of chitosan according to the procedures described above.

Carbon dots with or without N-doped (8 mg) were mixed with TiO2 (400 mg, Aladdin) in 20 mL ethanol. After 30-min ultrasound and 1-h magnetic stirring, the mixture was transferred into a Teflon autoclave at 200 °C for 10 h. Finally, the obtained powder was dried at 50 °C.

X-ray diffraction (XRD; ThermoFisher Scientific) was performed on DX2700 with Cu Kα radiation (40 kV, 30 mA) from 10° to 80°. The morphology and composition of samples were observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM; FEI Tecnai G2 F20) with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). The element compositions of samples were measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; ThermoFisher Scientific). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) measurements were detected with a scanning range of 500–4000 cm−1 using a Nicolet 5700 spectrometer (ThermoFisher Scientific, USA). The absorption spectra of the prepared samples were analyzing using the ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis; Biochrom) spectroscopy. Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were conducted with an Edinburgh FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer and excited at 260 nm. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was recorded on a Bruker A300 spectrometer. Photoelectrochemical experiments were carried out on a CHI–660C electrochemical workstation with a three-electrode system. The three-electrode system included working (fabricated photoelectrodes), counter (platinum plate foil), and reference (Ag/AgCl) electrodes. The 300 W Xenon lamp was used as a light source and 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution was used as the electrolyte solution. 3 mg sample was dispersed in 1 mL distilled water for the working electrodes. And then, the prepared slurry was dropped onto a conductive glass (FTO) sheet (1 cm × 2 cm) and dried at 80 °C.

The antifungal ability of samples (N-CDs, CDs, TiO2, N-CDs/TiO2, CDs/TiO2) against A. fumigatus was evaluated using spread plate assays. 1 mg samples were added into 1 × 104 CFU mL −1 A. fumigatus conidia suspension. Before light irritation, the mixture was stirred with a magnetic stirrer for 30 min in the dark to reach the adsorption equilibrium. The suspension was irradiated with a Xenon lamp for 30 min. During the exposure, circulating cooling water was used to ensure the photoreactor to maintain at 20 °C. Then the reaction solution was extracted at 5, 10, 15, and 30 min and spread on agar plates. The number of colonies was counted after 24 h of incubation at 37 °C. Fluorescence images of fungal mass were obtained using a fluorescence microscope (Zeiss Axio Vert, Germany) with calcofluor white (CFW) staining. Crystal violet (Solarbio, China) was applied to evaluate the inhibitory effect of samples on fungal biofilm formation and the absorption was measured at the wavelength of 570 nm.

Human corneal epithelial cells (HCECs; donated by Xiamen University, China) were grown in the DMEM/Hams F12 medium until 90 % confluency. Cells were co-cultured with samples (N-CDs, CDs, TiO2, N-CDs/TiO2, CDs/TiO2) with different concentrates (7.8, 15.6, 31.3, 62.5, 125, 250, 500,1000 μg/mL) in 96-well plates for 24 h. After sterile PBS washing, CCK-8 reagent was added into each well. The optical density was recorded at 450 nm. The fluorescence images of HCECs with Calcein and propidium iodide (PI) staining were photographed by confocal laser microscopy. The procedures of the EdU staining test were in accordance with the EdU cell proliferation kit (Beyotime, China). Briefly, HCECs were cultivated with 10 μM EdU reagent for 2 h and then stained with working solution and DAPI. The fluorescence images were collected under a Thermo Fisher microscope (China, 200 × ).

As presented in Fig. 1 a, N-CDs/TiO2 was synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Under 200 °C condition, the polymer chains of chitosan gradually broke down into small molecules, which undergo polymerization, cross-linking reactions, and further carbonization to form sp2 skeleton [24]. During this hydrothermal process, the amino groups in chitosan combine with the sp2 carbon skeleton to form C–N bonds and pyrrole N, thus completing the doping of N atoms and synthesis of N-CDs [25]. Subsequently, TiO2 and N-CDs were dispersed in ethanol solution and combined under 200 °C for 10 h. The carboxyl groups on the surface of N-CDs and the hydroxyl groups on the surface of TiO2 undergo condensation reaction to form Ti–O–C bond, which leads to the coupling between TiO2 and N-CDs. Moreover, the hydrothermal treatment could promote the efficient attachment and strong incorporation between N-CDs and TiO2 [26]. The morphology and compositions of N-CDs and N-CDs/TiO2 were characterized by HRTEM. The HRTEM image of N-CDs exhibits a spherical structure with average size of less than 10 nm (Fig. 1b). As shown in Fig. 1c, N-CDs/TiO2 exhibits spherical structures with a diameter ranging from 19 to 22 nm, which easily undergoes agglomeration [27]. The typical interplanar spacing of 0.352 nm is corresponded to anatase plane (101) of TiO2 (JCPDS card no. 21–1272) [28]. Moreover, the distribution of N-CDs can be seen on the surface of TiO2 (Fig. 1d), with the lattice distance of 0.21 nm is in accordance with the crystal plane (101) of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1e), proving the combination of TiO2 and N-CDs [29]. The EDS mapping images in Fig. 1f show that the C, N, O, and Ti elements are uniformly distributed on N-CDs/TiO2, further proving that the N-CDs/TiO2 has been successful synthesized and the surface of TiO2 is uniformly deposited with N-CDs.

XRD was carried out to determine the phase structural change of samples. Fig. S1a displays the XRD measurement of chitosan, in which the diffraction peak at 19.91° corresponded to the characteristic crystalline peak of chitosan [30]. Similarly, the diffraction peaks at 2θ of 13.6° and 21.9° are related to the (110) and (002) reflection peaks of sodium alginate, indicating that sodium alginate has a highly crystalline structure (Fig. S1b) [31]. As displayed in Fig. 2 a, the broad diffraction peaks at 2θ = 25.1° in the N-CDs and 2θ = 23.23° in the CDs are observed due to highly disordered carbon, which is coincided with the graphite lattice spacing in the HRTEM analysis. Meanwhile, the characteristic peaks of chitosan and sodium alginate diminish after carbonization [32,33]. The XRD patterns of TiO2, CDs/TiO2, and N-CDs/TiO2 present the (101), (103), (004), (200), (204), and (215) reflection peaks of anatase phase TiO2 [27,34]. However, no typical peaks of CDs and N-CDs could be observed in the CDs/TiO2 and N-CDs/TiO2. There are no obvious differences in the diffraction patterns of TiO2, CDs/TiO2, and N-CDs/TiO2, which is mainly attributed to the uniform dispersion and low content of carbon dots [35,36]. These results reveal that the presence of CDs and N-CDs had no effect on the phase structure of TiO2.

FT-IR was performed to characterize the functional groups of samples. As depicted in Fig. S2a, the absorption of C–H bending vibration corresponding to the pyran ring of chitosan is almost diminished in the spectrum of N-CDs, indicating the decomposition of chitosan pyranose rings and successful preparation of N-CDs [30]. Besides, the variation of the bands at 2920 cm−1 and 1570 cm−1 is related to the N–H bending vibration of the amino group. Compared with the FT-IR of sodium alginate, the absorption peak of C–O stretching vibration at 1090 cm−1 in the CDs obviously decrease, attributing to the depletion of hydroxyl groups after carbonization. Since the carbonization process forms the new C–H after CDs synthesis, the CO bending vibration at 1600 cm−1 and C–H vibration at 1410 cm−1 show slightly shift (Fig. S2b) [37]. Similarly, the surface chemical compositions and functional groups of TiO2, CDs/TiO2, and N-CDs/TiO2 are characterized using FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 2b, the slight absorption bands at 3370 cm−1 and 1630-1700 cm−1 are related to the surface hydroxyl group stretching vibration. The bands at 2960 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 in the CDs/TiO2 and N-CDs/TiO2 are associated with the stretching vibrations of C–O–C and C–H bonds, respectively. Compared with the FT-IR spectrum of CDs/TiO2, the doping of N atoms in the N-CDs/TiO2 can be confirmed by the stronger stretching vibrations of CO in amide and carboxyl groups located at 1630 cm−1. In addition, the change in the peaks of CDs/TiO2, and N-CDs/TiO2 below 1000 cm−1 compared to TiO2 could be observed in Fig. S3, suggesting that the coupling between carbon dots (N-CDs and CDs) and TiO2 is attributed to the formation of Ti–O–C bond [26,32].

XPS measurement was used to investigate the chemical and elemental compositions of N-CDs/TiO2. Fig. S4 displays the binding energy of C1s, N1s, O1s, and Ti2p, confirming the presence of C, N, O, and Ti elements. We further analyzed the XPS C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Ti 2p high-resolution spectra of N-CDs/TiO2. The C 1s spectra (Fig. 2c) show three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.1 eV, and 288.7 eV, which are attributed to C–C, C–N, and CO groups, respectively. The peak centered at 399.8 eV in the N 1s high-resolution spectrum confirms the predominance of pyrrole N in the N-CDs/TiO2 (Fig. 2d). The Ti 2p spectra (Fig. 2e) with two peaks at 458.7 eV and 464.5 eV are ascribed to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 respectively, which coincides with previous report that carbon dots had the potential to reduce Ti 2p3/2 into Ti 2p1/2 [38]. For the O 1s spectra of N-CDs/TiO2 (Fig. 2f), two distinguished peaks centered at binding energies of 529.9 eV and 531.4 eV correspond to Ti–O and CO, respectively. In accordance with the FT-IR results, these data also prove that TiO2 is successfully modified with N-CDs by the formation of Ti–O–C bond.

The photocatalytic fungi-deactivated properties of synthesized samples in our study against A. fumigatus were evaluated. A Xenon lamp (300 W) was used to simulate sunlight for the subsequent experiments. Fig. 3 a illustrates the excellent fungicidal effect of N-CDs/TiO2 against A. fumigatus on fungal structure through the production of ROSs and the increase of photocatalytic efficiency. As shown in Fig. 3b and g, the viability of A. fumigatus is apparently not affected under light exposure for 30 min in the absence of photocatalyst materials compared to the dark condition. The fungal inactivation ability of N-CDs starts to appear after 15 min of light irradiation and further enhances after 30 min. However, the addition of CDs exhibits the slight inhibitory effect on fungal growth under light irradiation. These results are consistent with previous finding that the N atoms doping could improve the photocatalytic performance of carbon dots [7]. Meanwhile, the bare TiO2 exerts negligible antifungal capacity after light stimulation. The fungicidal effect of CDs/TiO2 shows only slightly enhanced for 30 min of continuous light irradiation. Notably, the amount of destroyed fungi in N-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst after 10 min irradiation reach nearly 40 %, and no living fungi could be observed after 30-min light stimulation. Compared with CDs/TiO2 treatment, the fungicidal effect of N-CDs/TiO2 is increased by seven times after 10 min of light irradiation, and even reached 100 % fungal inactivation after 30 min. This verifies the superior photocatalytic fungicidal activity of N-CDs/TiO2 than CDs/TiO2 and TiO2, implying that N-CDs modified TiO2 composite nanomaterials can be considered as an excellent photocatalyst to damage fungi through sunlight.

To further verify the photocatalytic antifungal ability of prepared nanomaterials, fungal mass measurement and biofilm biomass assay were performed under 30-min exposure of a Xenon lamp light. CFW stain was used to dye fungal cell walls to detect the A. fumigatus hyphae growth [39]. As illustrated in Fig. 3c and e, no obvious difference in fungal mass is detected between the control and CDs-treated groups. N-CDs treatment with light irradiation could partially inhibit the germination of hyphae. Meanwhile, fluorescence images intuitively display that TiO2 has negligible influence on A. fumigatus hyphal growth even under the light condition. Notably, fewer stained hyphae are observed under the simulated sunlight irradiation in N-CDs/TiO2 treatment group compared with CDs/TiO2 treatment group due to the fact that N-doped carbon dots can endow semiconductor materials with better sunlight absorption [40]. Due to the critical role of biofilm formation in fungal invasion and resistance, the inhibitory effects of synthesized photocatalysts on A. fumigatus biofilm are determined via crystal violet staining [41]. Fig. 3d and f indicate that CDs have not influence on fungal biofilm formation, while N-CDs obviously decrease more biofilm mass under the light stimulation. Although CDs/TiO2 has the inhibitory effect on biofilm formation, N-CDs/TiO2 exhibits the enhanced antibiofilm property after 30 min of sunlight irradiation. But bare TiO2 shows no inhibitory impact on biofilm mass. These results further prove that the coupling of TiO2 with N-CDs exhibits higher photocatalytic fungicidal property under sunlight irradiation by blocking biofilm formation and hyphal growth.

To evaluate the long-term stability of N-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst, the synthesized N-CDs/TiO2 was placed at room temperature for 3 weeks, during which photocatalytic antifungal experiments were conducted weekly. As illustrated in Fig. S6, the number of viable fungi is obviously decreased under 15-min light exposure and the fungal inactivation of N-CDs/TiO2 reaches 100 % after 30 min of light irradiation. Furthermore, the antifungal efficacy of N-CDs/TiO2 is not reduced over the periods of 1, 2, and 3 weeks, suggesting that the antifungal property of the N-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst is stable over time and its potential for the practical applications. Moreover, to further highlight the superiority of N-CDs/TiO2, Table S1 exhibits the comparison of fungicidal efficiency between N-CDs/TiO2 and other photocatalytic antifungal materials. Compared with the previously published researches, the prepared N-CDs/TiO2 can achieve 100 % fungal deactivation under 30-min illumination, illustrating its rapid and efficient antifungal property. In addition, the fungicidal effect of N-CDs/TiO2 is mainly attributed to the strong oxidation of holes and ROSs (•OH and •O2 −) without the toxic metal ions release, which greatly ensured its safety.

To verify the relationship between N-doping concentration and photocatalytic antifungal effect of N-CDs/TiO2 materials, we chose three polysaccharides with varying N content as precursors for synthesizing carbon dots, which were used to combine with TiO2 to prepare photocatalysts (hyaluronic acid: N-CDs/TiO2 1, chitosan: N-CDs/TiO2 2, gelatin: N-CDs/TiO2 3). As the control group, the carbon dots without N-doped were synthesized using sodium alginate (CDs/TiO2). Since 2 % carbon dots were used to modify TiO2 in our study, we estimated the N content in carbon dots based on the nitrogen element analysis of different N-CDs/TiO2 (Table S2). As shown in Fig. S5, the spread plate assays show that compared with CDs/TiO2 materials, these three photocatalysts all exhibit stronger antifungal property after 15–30 min under light irradiation. In addition, with the N-doping concentration decreased, the photocatalytic antifungal effect of N-CDs/TiO2 becomes stronger. This probably due to the fact that excessive N atoms doping could mask the active site of carbon dots and produce a large number of defects as electron-hole recombination centers, thus inhibiting the antifungal performance of N-CDs/TiO2 [42,43].

ROSs are known to attack fungal cell membrane permeability and damage fungal genes, which plays a vital role in exerting the fungicidal capability. To explore the ROSs produced by N-CDs/TiO2 during the photocatalytic antifungal process, EPR tests were carried out. Under the light irradiation, the signals of DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•O2 − were observed to detect free radical species. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the intensity of DMPO-•OH in N-CDs/TiO2 is much higher than the CDs/TiO2 and TiO2, indicating that N-CDs/TiO2 generates more hydroxyl radicals (•OH) in process of the photocatalysis. Likewise, more superoxide radicals (•O2 −) are detected in the N-CDs/TiO2 under the light irradiation (Fig. 4b).

To further identify the primary reactive species in the photocatalytic antifungal process, we conducted spread plate assays with the addition of different scavengers, including potassium bromate (KBrO3) for electrons (e−), sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) for holes (h+), isopropanol (IPA) for •OH, and benzoquinone (BQ) for •O2 −. Fig. 4c illustrates that compared with the number of living fungi in bare N-CDs/TiO2 group under light irradiation, the addition of KBrO3 to capture e− obviously improves the photocatalytic antifungal efficiency. Meanwhile, almost no photocatalytic antifungal ability is observed with the addition of h+ capturer (Na2C2O4), indicating that h+ plays a pivotal role in enhancing photocatalytic antifungal efficiency of N-CDs/TiO2. During the photocatalytic antifungal process, the photoelectrons could react with O2 to produce •O2 − and the photogenerated h+ could oxidize H2O to •OH [44]. As shown in Fig. 4d, compared with the presence of BQ to capture •O2 −, the fungicidal effects are inhibited more obviously with the addition of the •OH capturer (IPA). This suggests that •OH is the main reaction intermediate in the mechanism of the photocatalytic antifungal process. The strong oxidation ability of ROSs and h+ can degrade fungal cell walls, inhibit the biofilm information, and severely damage the fungal genes and proteins, ultimately leading to fungal death [45].

Optical and electrochemical measurements were performed to verify the potential mechanism of the enhanced photocatalytic reaction in N-CDs/TiO2. To analyze the sunlight absorption ability of prepared photocatalysts, we conducted the UV–Vis spectroscopy. As depicted in Fig. S7, bare TiO2 shows absorption ability only in the ultraviolet region (200–400 nm) due to its wide bandgap. However, N-CDs exhibits stronger absorption in both ultraviolet and visible regions than CDs, indicating that N doping can significantly enhance the sunlight absorption capability of carbon dots. Similarly, the CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst possesses the enhanced visible light absorption, which can be further improved after the modification of TiO2 with N-CDs, suggesting that N-CDs can endow TiO2 with better sunlight absorption performance [46]. The charge separation efficiency in nanomaterials prepared in our study was examined using PL emission spectra. As shown in Fig. 5 a, N-CDs shows lower PL emission intensity than CDs, meanwhile the intensity of the N-CDs/TiO2 spectrum is lowest compared with the pure TiO2 and CDs/TiO2. Lower PL emission intensity indicates the higher electron-hole separation rate and reduced recombination in the photocatalytic reaction [47]. Thus, PL test results reveal that the modification of TiO2 by N-CDs effectively promotes the photogenerated electron-hole separation and inhibits the recombination, thus improving the photocatalytic efficiency.

The electron−hole pairs separation efficiency was directly verified by transient photocurrent response measurement. Under intermittent light irradiation with a Xenon lamp, CDs exhibit almost no photocurrent response, however the transient response of N-CDs is clearly improved (Fig. 5b). Furthermore, the addition of CDs and N-CDs on the TiO2 can obviously promote the photogenerated charge separation, with the photocurrent response of N-CDs/TiO2 being the highest. These results indicate that TiO2 modified with N-CDs could accelerate the photogenerated electron transfer efficiency and separation of electron-hole pairs in the photocatalytic process. Moreover, the Nyquist plots of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) reflects the electrode resistance and charge transfer efficiency. The smaller semi-arc radius indicates the higher charge transfer efficiency [48]. As shown in Fig. S8, the arc radius is obviously decreased after N-doped into CDs. Moreover, N-CDs/TiO2 exhibits the smallest arc radius compared with bare TiO2 and CDs/TiO2, indicating that the modification of TiO2 with N-CDs accelerates the charge transfer process. By analyzing the overpotentials and currents of all samples synthesized in our study, it can be seen in Fig. 5c that N-CDs/TiO2 displays the highest current density with the lowest overpotential, suggesting that the presence of N-CDs makes it faster to transfer the photogenerated electrons to TiO2 and promotes the photoreaction [49], which is consistent with the results of EIS measurement and transient photocurrent response. To further examine the electron transfer between TiO2 and N-CDs, we performed the high-resolution Ti 2p XPS spectra of N-CDs/TiO2 and TiO2 (Fig. S9). After modifying TiO2 with N-CDs, the Ti 2p3/2 peak shifts toward the lower binding energy (about 0.3 eV), indicating an increase in the surface electron density of TiO2 in the N-CDs/TiO2, which is ascribe to the electrons transferred from N-CDs to TiO2 after combination. This can promote the separation of photogenerated carriers [50].

The Tauc plots and Mott-Schottky plots were next used to analyze the band position and gap. Since the flat band potential of n-type semiconductor is normally positive 0.1 V than the conductive band (CB) potential [51]. As the Mott-Schottky plots at frequencies of 1000 Hz, 1500 Hz, and 2000 Hz shown in Fig. 5d, the CB potentials of TiO2, N-CDs/TiO2, CDs/TiO2, N-CDs, and CDs are −0.37 V, −0.56 V, −0.72 V, −0.43 V, and −0.4 V (versus. reversible hydrogen electrode, RHE), respectively. The bandgap energy (Eg) was calculated by the Tauc plots. As depicted in Fig. 5e and f, the Eg of N-CDs (1.52 eV) is distinctly narrower than that of CDs without N-doped (1.61 eV). Similarly, the calculated Eg of N-CDs/TiO2 (3.07 eV) is significantly lower than that of pure TiO2 (3.29 eV) and CDs/TiO2 (3.27 eV), demonstrating that TiO2 modified by N-CDs requires the less energy for the electron transition, thus promoting the photoactivation. And then, the valence band (VB) potentials of TiO2, N-CDs/TiO2, CDs/TiO2, N-CDs, and CDs are calculated to be 2.92 V, 2.47 V, 2.55 V, 1.09 V, and 1.21 V (according to the equation Eg = EVB - ECB), respectively [52]. Moreover, based on the results of XPS spectra, the VB potentials of TiO2, N-CDs/TiO2, CDs/TiO2, N-CDs, and CDs are 2.89 V, 2.44 V, 2.52 V, 1.03 V, and 1.20 V (Fig. S10). These results are approximately consistent with the VB values calculated based on Mott-Schottky and Tauc plots, further proving the narrower band gap in the N-CDs/TiO2 compared with TiO2 and CDs/TiO2. Fig. 5f presents that the CB electrons reduction potential of N-CDs/TiO2 was negative enough to reduce O2 to •O2 − and the VB holes possessed strong oxidation ability to oxidize water molecules and produce •OH [53,54].

Based on the above findings, this study exhibits the enhanced photocatalytic antifungal capacity of N-CDs/TiO2. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , during photocatalytic reaction, N-CDs/TiO2 can facilitate charge transition and the separation of electron-hole pairs by narrowing the band gap. Thus, under the sunlight irradiation, the photoelectrons are effectively captured while the h+ remains on the surface of N-CDs/TiO2. The photogenerated h+ could oxidize H2O to produce more •OH, which cooperates with the strong oxidation of h+ and •O2 − reduced by e− to enhance the photocatalytic antifungal capacity of N-CDs/TiO2.

To evaluate the biocompatibility of TiO2, CDs/TiO2, N-CDs/TiO2, CDs, and N-CDs, CCK-8 assays were carried out. HCECs were co-cultured with different samples for 24 h. As illustrated in Fig. 6 a and b, CDs inhibit the viability of 50 % cells when the concentration reaches 1000 μg/mL, while N-CDs at all concentrations ranging from 7.8 to 1000 μg/mL exert no negative effect on cell proliferation and growth. Fig. 6c exhibits the apparent cytotoxicity of TiO2. After the coupling of CDs with TiO2, cell survival rate is significantly increased, but nearly 20 % HCECs are damaged when the concentrations of CDs/TiO2 exceeded 62.5 μg/mL (Fig. 6d). Notably, the cytotoxicity of CDs/TiO2 is lower than that of bare CDs at 1000 μg/mL, primarily due to the increase in catalyst size after combination with TiO2, which hinders the entry into the cells and thus significantly reduced the cytotoxicity [55]. In addition, N-CDs/TiO2 at 7.8–1000 μg/mL have no evident inhibitory effect on HCECs, providing evidence for the great biocompatibility of N-CDs/TiO2 (Fig. 6e).

The safety of prepared samples was further examined by staining living cells with Calcein and dead cells with PI (Fig. 6f). Consistent with the results of CCK-8 tests, CDs, TiO2, and CDs/TiO2 treatments all lead to the partial cells death, with the toxicity of TiO2 being the highest. However, no dead cells are detected after incubation with N-CDs/TiO2 and N-CDs for 24 h. EdU staining was employed to assess whether synthesized catalysts influence the cell proliferation. As shown in Fig. 6g, the proportion of stained proliferative cells in N-CDs/TiO2 treatment is no difference with the control group and N-CDs group, which is clearly more than that of CDs-treated, TiO2-treated, and CDs/TiO2-treated groups. These results all prove the safety of N-CDs/TiO2 application, which is mainly contributed to the hydroxyl groups on the surface of N-CDs/TiO2. Consistent with previous reports, these hydrophilic groups can stably coexist with cells and reduce immune response, providing a suitable microenvironment for cell growth [21,32].

In this study, N-CDs/TiO2 composite nanomaterials were hydrothermally synthesized as a photocatalyst, which can better utilize the sunlight irradiation to effectively inhibit the A. fumigatus hyphal growth and biofilm formation by the strong oxidation of holes and ROSs (•OH and •O2 −). Compared with the CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst, N-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst significantly enhanced the photocatalytic reaction efficiency via lowering the energy band gap and promoting the separation of photogenerated electron/hole pairs, thereby improving the utilization of sunlight to exerting enhanced antifungal effect. Moreover, the coupling of N-CDs with TiO2 could significantly improve the biocompatibility of TiO2. These findings indicate that N-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst can be considered as a potential and effective antifungal agent to better use sunlight against corneal fungal infections.

Menghui Chi: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Lingwen Gu: Supervision, Data curation. Ke Liu: Resources, Methodology. Jing Lin: Resources, Project administration. Qian Wang: Supervision, Resources. Bing Yu: Methodology. Ziyi Wang: Investigation. Xueyun Fu: Methodology. Daohao Li: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Investigation, Conceptualization. Guiqiu Zhao: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Cui Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
